ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Framing automatic dialogue evaluation as a learned supervised predictor (ADEM) and providing a new human-scored dataset represents a notable and useful perspective shift relative to hand-crafted word-overlap metrics. The method combines existing neural representations (VHRED, PCA) and training procedures, so the technical novelty is a non-trivial but expected extension rather than a radical new algorithmic advance. The paper clearly differentiates itself from BLEU/METEOR-style baselines and demonstrates generalization to unseen dialogue models, which would be informative to experts. However, reliance on pretrained representation pipelines and lack of theoretical guarantees keeps it from being highly original. | B(4): The paper advances a notable, practical new perspective by formulating automatic dialogue evaluation as a supervised learning problem and introducing ADEM, which predicts human-like scores and demonstrably outperforms word-overlap metrics. Training a neural evaluator on a newly collected human-scored dataset and leveraging pretrained dialogue representations (VHRED) is a non-trivial step beyond prior BLEU/METEOR-style or simple retrieval baselines, and the claim of generalization to unseen dialogue models further differentiates it. While the core idea of learned evaluators is intuitive, the paper’s combination of model, dataset, and empirical validation would inform and surprise practitioners used to relying on overlap metrics, so I judge it notably novel rather than purely incremental.

reviewer_a.justification: Framing automatic dialogue evaluation as a learned supervised predictor (ADEM) and providing a new human-scored dataset represents a notable and useful perspective shift relative to hand-crafted word-overlap metrics. The method combines existing neural representations (VHRED, PCA) and training procedures, so the technical novelty is a non-trivial but expected extension rather than a radical new algorithmic advance. The paper clearly differentiates itself from BLEU/METEOR-style baselines and demonstrates generalization to unseen dialogue models, which would be informative to experts. However, reliance on pretrained representation pipelines and lack of theoretical guarantees keeps it from being highly original.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper advances a notable, practical new perspective by formulating automatic dialogue evaluation as a supervised learning problem and introducing ADEM, which predicts human-like scores and demonstrably outperforms word-overlap metrics. Training a neural evaluator on a newly collected human-scored dataset and leveraging pretrained dialogue representations (VHRED) is a non-trivial step beyond prior BLEU/METEOR-style or simple retrieval baselines, and the claim of generalization to unseen dialogue models further differentiates it. While the core idea of learned evaluators is intuitive, the paper’s combination of model, dataset, and empirical validation would inform and surprise practitioners used to relying on overlap metrics, so I judge it notably novel rather than purely incremental.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is overall well organized and follows a clear progression from problem motivation to method (ADEM) and experiments, with contributions stated early (learning an automatic evaluator, new human-scored dataset). Notation and methodological detail appear consistent enough to reproduce (hyperparameters reported, optimization details given), and figures/tables/examples are reported as effective. My reservations are minor presentation gaps: the paper could more explicitly summarize dataset collection and subsampling decisions in one place for readers focusing on experimental validity, and the reliance on pretrained VHRED representations would benefit from clearer ablation pointers—nevertheless the writing quality and related-work coverage are strong. | B(4): The paper is generally well organized and the main contributions (formulating learned evaluation, introducing ADEM, and releasing a human-scored dataset) are clearly stated early. Notation and methodological description are consistent, and experiments/hyperparameters are reported in sufficient detail; related work coverage is thorough. Figures, tables, and examples are informative and support the claims, and the writing quality is good overall, though a couple minor presentation issues (e.g., explicit statement about code availability and slightly terse discussion of some training details) prevent an exceptional rating.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is overall well organized and follows a clear progression from problem motivation to method (ADEM) and experiments, with contributions stated early (learning an automatic evaluator, new human-scored dataset). Notation and methodological detail appear consistent enough to reproduce (hyperparameters reported, optimization details given), and figures/tables/examples are reported as effective. My reservations are minor presentation gaps: the paper could more explicitly summarize dataset collection and subsampling decisions in one place for readers focusing on experimental validity, and the reliance on pretrained VHRED representations would benefit from clearer ablation pointers—nevertheless the writing quality and related-work coverage are strong.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is generally well organized and the main contributions (formulating learned evaluation, introducing ADEM, and releasing a human-scored dataset) are clearly stated early. Notation and methodological description are consistent, and experiments/hyperparameters are reported in sufficient detail; related work coverage is thorough. Figures, tables, and examples are informative and support the claims, and the writing quality is good overall, though a couple minor presentation issues (e.g., explicit statement about code availability and slightly terse discussion of some training details) prevent an exceptional rating.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper tackles an important, practical problem—automatic evaluation of non-task dialogue—by proposing ADEM, a learned evaluator that empirically correlates better with human judgements than BLEU/METEOR/ROUGE and generalizes to unseen dialogue models, and by releasing a human-scored dataset. From my perspective, the contribution is significant because it shifts evaluation from brittle word-overlap metrics to data-driven learned evaluators and demonstrates utility on real Twitter/HRED/VHRED setups. I withhold a transformative rating because the approach depends heavily on pretrained VHRED representations, requires supervised human scores (limiting scalability), and lacks theoretical analysis or comprehensive ablations that would clarify when such learned evaluators truly generalize. Nonetheless, this work should strongly influence subsequent research and tool development in dialogue evaluation. | B(4): This paper tackles a central practical bottleneck—automatic evaluation of non-task dialogue—by reframing it as a supervised learning problem and introducing ADEM, which empirically correlates much better with human judgments than BLEU/METEOR/ROUGE and even generalizes to unseen systems. The release of a new human-scored dataset and concrete training details makes the approach directly adoptable by practitioners and likely to spur follow-up work on learned evaluators. While not fully transformative (it still depends on human-labeled data and has limitations like length bias), it meaningfully shifts evaluation practice and opens substantial new research directions.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper tackles an important, practical problem—automatic evaluation of non-task dialogue—by proposing ADEM, a learned evaluator that empirically correlates better with human judgements than BLEU/METEOR/ROUGE and generalizes to unseen dialogue models, and by releasing a human-scored dataset. From my perspective, the contribution is significant because it shifts evaluation from brittle word-overlap metrics to data-driven learned evaluators and demonstrates utility on real Twitter/HRED/VHRED setups. I withhold a transformative rating because the approach depends heavily on pretrained VHRED representations, requires supervised human scores (limiting scalability), and lacks theoretical analysis or comprehensive ablations that would clarify when such learned evaluators truly generalize. Nonetheless, this work should strongly influence subsequent research and tool development in dialogue evaluation.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles a central practical bottleneck—automatic evaluation of non-task dialogue—by reframing it as a supervised learning problem and introducing ADEM, which empirically correlates much better with human judgments than BLEU/METEOR/ROUGE and even generalizes to unseen systems. The release of a new human-scored dataset and concrete training details makes the approach directly adoptable by practitioners and likely to spur follow-up work on learned evaluators. While not fully transformative (it still depends on human-labeled data and has limitations like length bias), it meaningfully shifts evaluation practice and opens substantial new research directions.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper presents a clear claim and provides convincing empirical evidence that ADEM correlates better with human judgments than word-overlap metrics, but they also note missing statistical tests, limited ablations, and reliance on pretrained VHRED and supervised data. These are important limitations but are largely methodological rather than fatal to the contribution, so a score of 4 reflects solid empirical contribution with some minor technical issues that should be addressed in revision.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly states its main claim (ADEM learns to predict human-like scores) and provides experimental evidence showing higher Pearson correlations than BLEU/METEOR/ROUGE and comparisons against several neural baselines (TF-IDF, Dual Encoder, HRED, VHRED). However, from a theory-first perspective the work relies heavily on pretrained VHRED representations with limited ablation or theoretical justification for generalization, and it's unclear whether statistical tests and sufficiently diverse non-neural baselines were used to fully validate robustness. The authors acknowledge length bias and mitigate it via subsampling, but claims of generalization to unseen models would benefit from more diagnostic experiments and explicit failure-mode analysis.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claim—that framing dialogue evaluation as a learned prediction problem via ADEM yields substantially higher correlation with human judgements than word-overlap metrics—is supported by experiments on the new human-scored dataset and comparisons to reasonable baselines (BLEU/METEOR/ROUGE, retrieval and neural models). There are no complex derivations to check, and the methodology is largely sound (pretraining with VHRED, PCA reduction, length-based subsampling), but the summary does not mention significance testing or code release, and ADEM's reliance on a supervised dataset is an important practical limitation. Overall the empirical evidence appears convincing though a few standard experimental-controls/statistical analyses are missing, so I judge it as having minor technical issues that do not overturn the main conclusions.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important problem, introduces a useful learned evaluator (ADEM), and provides a new human-scored dataset with thorough empirical comparisons to sensible baselines. However, Reviewer A’s concerns about limited ablations of pretrained components and lack of theoretical or diagnostic analysis temper enthusiasm for unquestioned top-tier acceptance; consequently I assign a slightly conservative score reflecting strong empirical fit but room for deeper analysis and component-level evaluation.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper sits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML scope: it addresses dialogue evaluation, introduces a learned evaluator (ADEM), and provides a new human-scored dataset while comparing to standard baselines (BLEU/METEOR/ROUGE, TF-IDF, DE, HRED, VHRED). From my theory-first perspective, I appreciate the empirical comparisons and length-bias mitigation, but I am concerned about reliance on pretrained VHRED representations with limited ablation and lack of theoretical analysis of when a learned evaluator will generalize—issues that make it a strong but not perfect fit for the highest-tier venues without additional diagnostics. Overall it targets the right audience and uses expected methods/metrics, but warrants stronger component-level analysis to fully satisfy top-conference standards.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it addresses an important evaluation problem in dialogue systems by formulating automatic evaluation as a supervised learning task and introduces ADEM, which shows substantially improved correlation with human judgements compared to BLEU/METEOR/ROUGE and generalizes to unseen systems. The work includes a newly-collected human-scored dataset, thorough comparisons to sensible baselines (TF-IDF, DE, HRED, VHRED, tweet2vec variant) and reports relevant experimental details (hyperparameters, mitigation of length bias), matching the empirical and reproducibility standards I expect. Given the relevance to the dialogue and evaluation communities and the practical impact of a learned evaluator, this paper is highly appropriate for the venue.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper compares ADEM to a strong, relevant set of baselines, evaluates on the same human-scored Twitter data, and addresses dataset/training biases, making the experimental comparisons meaningful and reproducible. Reviewer A’s point that a few additional non-overlap or embedding-based automatic metrics / simpler supervised regressors could further strengthen the claim is reasonable, so I award a 4 to reflect strong but not absolutely exhaustive comparisons.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares ADEM against a broad set of relevant baselines including traditional word-overlap metrics (BLEU, METEOR, ROUGE), a simple TF-IDF retrieval model, and several neural dialogue models (Dual Encoder, HRED, VHRED), and even an ablation using tweet2vec—so the comparisons are generally strong and the authors address known biases (e.g., length) when training. From my theory-first perspective, I note minor omissions: the summary does not indicate inclusion of non-neural or learned non-overlap automatic metrics (e.g., embedding-based or simpler supervised regressors) that would strengthen claims of superiority, nor is it explicit that all methods were evaluated under identical conditions (though Pearson correlation with human judgements is used). Overall the experimental comparisons are good but not fully exhaustive, hence a 4.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares ADEM to a strong set of baselines (word-overlap metrics BLEU/METEOR/ROUGE, retrieval TF-IDF, neural models Dual Encoder/HRED/VHRED and an embedding variant ADEM(T2V)) and evaluates all on the same Twitter data with Pearson correlations to human judgements. Training details and dataset biases (length bias) are acknowledged and mitigated, and the authors report generalization to unseen systems, indicating fair conditions. Related work coverage is described as thorough and the paper provides a new human-scored dataset, enabling reproducible and meaningful comparisons—so the empirical comparisons are comprehensive and appropriate.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes a solid, practically relevant empirical contribution (new human-scored dataset, improved correlations over standard metrics, and some generalization to unseen models) but falls short on deeper ablation/sensitivity analyses and explicit release of code/artifacts. Given these strengths and the acknowledged limitations (length bias, reliance on pretrained representations, limited component-level diagnostics), a score of 4 reflects the paper’s strong experimental value while noting areas needing further analysis and reproducibility improvements.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides a reasonable empirical evaluation (new human-scored dataset, multiple baselines including non-neural and neural models, and system-level/utterance-level correlations) and addresses a clear failure mode (length bias) with subsampling. However, from a theory-first perspective it lacks principled analysis of when a learned evaluator like ADEM will generalize beyond seen dialogue models and depends heavily on pretrained VHRED representations with limited component-level ablation (only a tweet2vec variant reported). Reproducibility is weakened because code release is not mentioned, and while related work is well covered, the paper would benefit from deeper sensitivity analyses and explicit diagnostics of failure modes beyond length bias.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents solid empirical validation: it trains ADEM on a newly collected human-scored dataset, shows substantially improved utterance- and system-level Pearson correlations over BLEU/METEOR/ROUGE, and demonstrates generalization to unseen dialogue models. It reports training details and hyperparameters and acknowledges important limitations (length bias and need for human-scored data), but the summary suggests only limited ablation/sensitivity analyses beyond length-based subsampling and one embedding variant (ADEM (T2V)). Code availability is not mentioned, which somewhat weakens reproducibility. Overall the experiments are strong and practically relevant but miss some deeper ablations and explicit release of artifacts.

main_claims: Formulate automatic dialogue evaluation as a learning problem and introduce an evaluation model (ADEM) that predicts human-like scores for dialogue responses.
ADEM’s predictions correlate significantly with human judgements and substantially better than word-overlap metrics such as BLEU, METEOR, and ROUGE at both utterance and system levels.
ADEM can generalize to evaluating dialogue models unseen during training.
Provide a new dataset of human response scores for training and evaluating learned evaluators.
_summary.methodology: Train a neural evaluation model (ADEM) to predict human scores for candidate responses using representations (pretrained with VHRED and reduced via PCA), with training procedures that include length-based subsampling to reduce length bias and optimization details (e.g., Adam).
datasets: A newly-collected dataset of human response scores (new dataset introduced in the paper)
limitations: Existing automatic word-overlap metrics are biased and correlate poorly with human judgements.
Human ratings exhibit length bias (shorter responses tend to receive higher ratings), requiring subsampling/mitigation when training ADEM.
ADEM requires a dataset of human response scores for supervised training.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - dialogue evaluation / non-task-oriented dialogue systems

reviewer_a.name: Theory-First Skeptic
reviewer_a.background: Tenured researcher in machine learning and theoretical NLP, with a track record of work on formal evaluation metrics and generalization theory.
traits: penalises papers that present empirical models without theoretical justification or guarantees (e.g., no analysis of when learned evaluators generalize)
penalises reliance on pretrained representation pipelines with limited ablation (requires explicit component-level ablations for claims of generalization)
requires at least three diverse, well-motivated baselines (including simple baselines and non-neural methods) or strong analysis explaining their exclusion
downgrades papers that do not provide clear failure modes or limitations (expects explicit discussion and diagnostic experiments)
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: I prioritize theoretical grounding, clear ablation/diagnostic analysis, and rigorous arguments about generalization; I score up innovations only when accompanied by principled justification and comprehensive comparisons.

reviewer_b.name: Practical Empirical Champion
reviewer_b.background: Senior NLP researcher focusing on dialogue systems and evaluation, with extensive experience building and benchmarking conversational models and shared datasets.
traits: rewards papers that introduce a clear, useful new problem formulation or tool (e.g., learned automatic evaluators) and provide empirical evidence that it outperforms common baselines
gives credit for releasing datasets and annotations that enable reproducibility and future work
favours strong empirical correlation improvements on human judgements and system-level analyses, even if theoretical justification is limited
values demonstration of generalization to unseen systems/models and practical training details that practitioners can adopt
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: I evaluate papers primarily on the novelty and practical impact of the idea, the quality and breadth of empirical validation, and whether the artifacts (data/code) will advance the community.
